source: Learning Rsync - rsync brief description: rsync in brief: why rsync, and its basic principles and features

questions.q: Why not just use tar/cp plus scp/sftp for production backups?
options: tar/cp is slow and heavy, scp/sftp uses a lot of bandwidth, and manual crontab timing is hard to get right
questions.why: In production those consume time, CPU and bandwidth, and need manual scheduling that is hard to tune.

questions.q: Which three requirements should a production backup meet (that rsync addresses)?
options: Transmitted over the network, near real-time file synchronization, and low resource use / high efficiency
Encryption, a GUI, and cloud storage
questions.why: The book lists network transfer, real-time sync, and efficient low-resource operation as the needs rsync meets.
rsync is a fast incremental backup tool under the GNU open-source licence.

questions.q: Does rsync itself perform real-time synchronization?
options: No -- it is incremental only; real-time needs a helper such as inotify
questions.why: rsync alone does not do real-time sync; it must be supplemented by another program.

options: One-way by default (two-way needs another tool such as unison)
questions.why: Sync is one-directional; bidirectional sync requires cooperating tools like unison.

questions.q: Which two protocols can rsync use to transfer data?
options: The SSH protocol and the rsync protocol
questions.why: rsync transfers over either SSH or its own rsync protocol.

questions.q: What is the default port of the rsync protocol?
questions.why: The rsync protocol listens on port 873 (SSH uses 22).

questions.q: Which file attributes can rsync selectively preserve?
options: Hard/soft links, owner, group, permissions, and modification time
questions.why: rsync can preserve links, owner, group, permissions, mtime and more, recursively over a directory.
